perturbation.original_text: Galacticomm must provide a person(s) to correct any technical problems (Server being down or inaccessible) 24 hours per day, 7 days per week. This person(s) must be available by beeper or telephone.
perturbation.changed_text: Galacticomm should endeavor to provide a person(s) to address technical problems (Server being down or inaccessible) during business hours. This person(s) should be reachable by standard communication methods.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "must provide" to "should endeavor to provide" weakens the obligation to provide 24/7 technical support. Limiting the availability to "business hours" and changing "beeper or telephone" to "standard communication methods" introduces ambiguity and makes it difficult to enforce the original level of immediate response.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes Chapter 501, specifically concerning deceptive and unfair trade practices if the reduced support leads to the service not meeting reasonable consumer expectations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 501.204 prohibits unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. The modified text allows Galacticomm to significantly reduce its support obligations. If this reduction leads to the service being unreliable, and if the original agreement implied 24/7 support, it could be considered a deceptive practice as customers would not be receiving the level of service they were led to believe they would receive.

F.S. 501.204 501.204 Unlawful acts and practices. — (1) Unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (2) It is the intent of the Legislature that, in construing subsection (1), due consideration and great weight shall be given to the interpretations of the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts relating to s. 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act, 15 U.S.C. s. 45(a)(1) as of July 1, 2006. History. — s. 1, ch. 73-124; s. 1, ch. 83-117; s. 4, ch. 85-63; s. 2, ch. 90-190; s. 3, ch. 93-38; s. 2, ch. 2001-39; s. 23, ch. 2001-214; s. 2, ch. 2006-196.
perturbation.justification: The change from "must provide" to "should endeavor to provide" significantly weakens the obligation for 24/7 technical support, creating ambiguity in the agreement. The limitation to "business hours" further reduces the original commitment. According to Florida Statutes § 501.204, "Unfair methods of competition, unconscionable acts or practices, and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful." The modified text could be interpreted as a deceptive practice if customers were initially led to believe they would receive 24/7 support, but the new agreement only guarantees support during business hours. This undermines the original implied agreement of continuous service.

perturbation.original_text: Any disputes arising herein shall be settled in a court in FLorida.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes arising herein may be subject to resolution through methods deemed appropriate within Florida.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates dispute resolution in Florida courts. The modified clause replaces this with a vague statement that disputes "may be subject to resolution through methods deemed appropriate within Florida." This removes the certainty of court adjudication and opens the door to potentially unfair or biased alternative dispute resolution processes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes Chapter 682, specifically regarding arbitration agreements and their enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 682.02 outlines the validity of arbitration agreements. The modified clause, by removing the requirement for court settlement, implies potential arbitration or mediation. However, without clear guidelines on the method or selection process, one party could potentially force the other into an unfair arbitration process, which might not meet the standards required for enforceability under Florida law if the agreement is unconscionable or lacks mutuality.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 682 > Section 02 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 682 ARBITRATION CODE Entire Chapter SECTION 02 Arbitration agreements made valid, irrevocable, and enforceable; scope. F.S. 682.02 682.02 Arbitration agreements made valid, irrevocable, and enforceable; scope. — (1) An agreement contained in a record to submit to arbitration any existing or subsequent controversy arising between the parties to the agreement is valid, enforceable, and irrevocable except upon a ground that exists at law or in equity for the revocation of a contract. (2) The court shall decide whether an agreement to arbitrate exists or a controversy is subject to an agreement to arbitrate. (3) An arbitrator shall decide whether a condition precedent to arbitrability has been fulfilled and whether a contract containing a valid agreement to arbitrate is enforceable. (4) If a party to a judicial proceeding challenges the existence of, or claims that a controversy is not subject to, an agreement to arbitrate, the arbitration proceeding may continue pending final resolution of the issue by the court, unless the court otherwise orders. (5) This section also applies to written interlocal agreements under ss. 163.01 and 373.713 in which two or more parties agree to submit to arbitration any controversy between them concerning water use permit applications and other matters, regardless of whether or not the water management district with jurisdiction over the subject application is a party to the interlocal agreement or a participant in the arbitration. History. — s. 1, ch. 57-402; s. 12, ch. 67-254; s. 3, ch. 98-402; s. 26, ch. 2010-205; s. 7, ch. 2013-232. Note. — Former s. 57.11.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates disputes to be settled in a court in Florida. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating disputes "may be subject to resolution through methods deemed appropriate within Florida," removing the certainty of court adjudication. The Law Explanation suggests this could lead to unfair arbitration, potentially violating Florida Statute 682.02. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing F.S. 682.02, states that an agreement to arbitrate must be valid, enforceable, and irrevocable, except on grounds for revocation of a contract. By making dispute resolution methods vague, the changed text opens the door to potentially unfair processes not meeting the standards for enforceability under Florida law, contradicting the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Horst Entertainment shall send this amount to Galacticomm by no later than Wednesday for accesses used from the previuos week (Monday thru Sunday).
perturbation.changed_text: Horst Entertainment should remit payment to Galacticomm in a timely fashion for accesses accrued.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear payment deadline (Wednesday) for the previous week's accesses. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing "shall send this amount by no later than Wednesday" with "should remit payment in a timely fashion." The term "timely fashion" is vague and open to interpretation, and "accesses accrued" is similarly indefinite, potentially leading to payment delays and disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida's Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), specifically Article 2 relating to the sale of goods (in this case, web hosting services which can be construed as 'goods' due to the software usage).
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 672.309 addresses the absence of specific time provisions in contracts for sale. While web hosting is a service, the agreement mentions software usage and bandwidth, which can be interpreted as akin to goods. The UCC implies a reasonable time for payment when not specified. However, removing the clear deadline (Wednesday) can lead to disputes about what constitutes a 'reasonable time,' potentially violating the UCC's principles of good faith and commercial reasonableness if payments are significantly delayed due to the ambiguous language. The changes also weaken the original agreement's clear payment terms, which could be seen as an unfair or deceptive practice, especially if Galacticomm leverages this ambiguity to claim late fees or other penalties.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 309 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 309 Absence of specific time provisions; notice of termination. F.S. 672.309 672.309 Absence of specific time provisions; notice of termination. — (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this chapter or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-309, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a specific payment deadline (Wednesday) with the vague term "timely fashion." This directly contradicts Florida Statute § 672.309, which states that if a contract does not specify a time for performance, it defaults to a "reasonable time." While "timely fashion" might be interpreted as "reasonable time," the removal of the explicit deadline introduces ambiguity and increases the potential for disputes over what constitutes a reasonable payment timeframe. This ambiguity weakens the original agreement and could lead to breaches of contract.
